name: Linked List
def: A sequence of nodes each holding a value and a pointer to the next (and optionally previous) node.
problem: O(1) insertion/removal at a known position without shifting elements, when indices are not needed.
mechanism: Nodes are individually heap-allocated and linked by pointers; singly-linked lists chain forward, doubly-linked also backward for O(1) removal given a node.
benefits: O(1) splice/insert/remove at a held node
failures: Cache-miss-bound traversal far slower than arrays
whenNot: Indexed access or iteration speed matters (use a dynamic array)
Almost always — arrays win in practice
myths: "Lists beat arrays for inserts" — array shifts are usually faster than the cache misses lists incur

rust.note: ownership makes them painful; use Vec
